Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic even though I managed to get good grades. I'd listen to lectures and podcasts while doing other things—cooking, commuting, even just lying in bed. I rarely sat down at a desk to study in a traditional sense. Most of my learning happened in audio format because that's just how information sticks for me. I'd cram in study sessions whenever I could, not really following a set schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how scared I was about falling behind. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd have a marathon session when a deadline loomed. Being 26 and having other life stuff going on meant I couldn't always stick to a regular schedule. The fear of failing would kick in and that's when I'd really buckle down.
Q3: Usually it was panic, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or realize I hadn't checked in for a while and feel this wave of fear wash over me. Sometimes the gamified elements would draw me back—seeing progress bars or earning points gave me a small dopamine hit. But mostly it was deadline anxiety that got me logging in.
Q4: Not really a routine in the traditional sense, but the gamified tasks made it almost addictive at times. I'd find myself checking my progress or seeing if I'd unlocked anything new. It became like checking social media—not scheduled, but habitual in a scattered way. The game elements definitely helped make it feel less like a chore and more like something I wanted to peek at.
Q5: The video content was essential for me—I watched over 200 minutes worth because I could listen while doing other things. Audio-based resources and recorded lectures were my lifeline since I learn best by hearing information. The discussion posts were also helpful because hearing other perspectives reinforced concepts for me. Anything I could passively absorb while multitasking felt most valuable.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and text-heavy PDFs. My brain just doesn't process written information as efficiently, so I'd skim them at best or skip them entirely. Some of the peer interaction features felt awkward to me, so I only engaged minimally there. If something required me to sit and read for long periods, I'd put it off indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be assessed on or if it had that gamified element that made it feel rewarding. I also valued anything I could listen to rather than read. If I could see immediate progress or get some kind of feedback, that motivated me to complete it. Busy work without clear purpose felt like a waste.
Q8: Optional activities felt like bonus rounds in a video game—nice to have but not essential. I'd tackle them when I was feeling motivated or wanted extra points in the gamified system. Required activities carried this weight of fear behind them, like I absolutely couldn't skip them without consequences. The optional stuff was more relaxed, less pressure, but I still did quite a bit of it because of the game mechanics.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of audio files and lectures to my phone so I could listen offline during my commute or while doing chores. I also used some external podcasts and YouTube videos that explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me. A lot of my actual learning happened with headphones in, away from any screen that could track what I was doing.
Q10: There were countless hours where I was listening to downloaded lectures while walking or doing housework—none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also replay certain audio segments multiple times to really understand them, but the LMS would only show I accessed it once. My most productive study sessions were often completely invisible because they happened offline with downloaded content.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it maybe three or four times over the following days. The LMS would show one access, but I'd have absorbed that material deeply through repetition. It was more efficient for me to have everything on my phone rather than streaming it repeatedly. My offline engagement was way higher than my online presence suggested.
Q12: There were definitely periods where fear paralyzed me instead of motivating me. When assignments piled up or I felt overwhelmed by the workload, I'd freeze and avoid the LMS entirely. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me hard and made it difficult to bounce back quickly. Those low attendance numbers reflect times when I just couldn't bring myself to engage.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage—at 26, I have more going on than just school. When work or personal stuff got intense, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Fatigue was a big factor too; some days I just couldn't focus on a screen but could still listen to audio content. The stress would build up and I'd feel too overwhelmed to even log in.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd be logged in, maybe clicking through pages, but my mind was elsewhere—worried about other things or just too tired to focus. It felt like going through the motions without actually learning. Those sessions probably looked productive in the data but were basically empty engagement.
Q15: I postponed reading-heavy assignments constantly because they felt so daunting to me as an auditory learner. Even knowing they were important, I'd convince myself I'd do them later and then scramble at the last minute. The fear of starting something difficult was sometimes stronger than the fear of the deadline. I'd avoid anything that felt cognitively overwhelming until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd coast along barely engaging, then suddenly become hyper-focused when something was due soon. The gamified elements helped spread things out a bit, but ultimately that deadline fear was what drove most of my activity spikes. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more than I already did.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced me, especially when it was positive. Good grades or encouraging comments made me feel like maybe I could actually do this, which boosted my mood and motivation. Negative feedback would sometimes scare me into working harder, but it could also make me want to avoid the course entirely. The gamified feedback—points, badges, progress indicators—kept me coming back more consistently.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging for me. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, but the flexibility also meant I could drift and procrastinate. The gamified elements added structure that I actually enjoyed and responded to. Without those game mechanics, I probably would have been way less engaged overall.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement so much easier—they turned studying into something almost fun. Having audio and video content available was crucial for my learning style. What made it harder was the amount of reading required and the expectation of regular attendance, which just didn't fit my life. The peer interaction requirements also felt uncomfortable and forced for me.
Q20: I think some students might be genuinely trying but struggling to produce quality work, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. They could also be exploring the LMS out of curiosity or fear of missing something important. Sometimes high activity is just nervous energy—logging in to feel productive without actually accomplishing much. It might also be a sign of confusion about what's actually required.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. I did most of my meaningful learning offline, listening to downloaded content repeatedly. My low attendance and sporadic logins didn't show the hours I spent with audio materials away from the LMS. You can absolutely master content without being visibly active online if you have the right resources downloaded. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long you're on a page. But they miss the depth of engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm actually absorbing information or just staring blankly at a screen. They definitely can't capture offline learning, re-listening to audio, or the mental processing that happens when I'm not connected. Logs show presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means the material is actually getting into my brain and making sense, regardless of where or how I'm studying. It's about feeling connected to the content and motivated to understand it, not just completing tasks. For me, engagement happens most when I'm listening and processing, even if I'm doing something else simultaneously. It's an internal state that doesn't always match external behavior.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much time I spent listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, or how I'd think about concepts while doing completely unrelated activities. It wouldn't capture the fear that both motivated and paralyzed me, or how the gamified elements genuinely changed my relationship with the course. My best learning happened in earbuds, offline, invisible to any tracking system. The data shows a disengaged drifter, but I was actually deeply engaged—just not in ways the platform could see.
